pattern: Because one bucket placed between two hamsters can feed both at once, always preferring to feed a hamster from the cell ahead of it rather than the cell behind it leaves the most future flexibility, which makes a single greedy left-to-right pass optimal.
hints: Only empty cells can ever hold a bucket, and a hamster is satisfied the moment a bucket sits directly to its left or its right, so scan left to right and only act when you reach a hamster that isn't already satisfied by a bucket placed earlier.

When an unsatisfied hamster needs a bucket, prefer placing it in the empty cell just after the hamster rather than just before it -- a forward bucket can still go on to feed the next hamster too, while a backward one never helps anything later.

Only fall back to the cell just before the hamster if the cell just after it isn't available (out of bounds or already occupied by another hamster); if neither side offers an empty cell, feeding every hamster is impossible and the answer is -1.

edge_inputs.description: two separated hamsters each needing their own bucket
one bucket placed between two hamsters feeds both at once
a hamster flanked by hamsters on both sides has no empty cell available at all
two adjacent hamsters with no gap between them or room outside
a single hamster and nothing else, no adjacent cell exists in bounds
a hamster flush against the very start of the string
a hamster flush against the very end of the string
a strictly alternating pattern where a naive one-bucket-per-hamster approach would overcount
a long run of empty cells around a single hamster needs only one bucket

stress.description: random hamster strings of varying density, sized up to a few thousand characters, for the optimal linear-scan solution
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 4000))
    density = rng.choice([0.2, 0.35, 0.5, 0.65, 0.8])
    s = ''.join('H' if rng.random() < density else '.' for _ in range(n))
    return [s]

stress.description: random hamster strings capped at a short length so an exponential brute-force cross-check would still stay fast
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 20))
    density = rng.choice([0.2, 0.35, 0.5, 0.65, 0.8])
    s = ''.join('H' if rng.random() < density else '.' for _ in range(n))
    return [s]
stress.note: kept short (<=20 chars) so an exponential subset-based oracle stays fast if ever exercised against it
